content.problemStatement:
Write a program to check if the given number NNN is Armstrong number.

**Note:** Armstrong number is a number that is equal to the sum of its own digits each raised to the power of the number of digits.


Input Format

The first and only line of input contains a single integer, NNN - The number that needs to be checked.


Output Format

Print on a single line:

`YES` if NNN is Armstrong number.
`NO` otherwise.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (0 ≤ N ≤ 10^6).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print "YES" if N is an Armstrong number, otherwise print "NO". Each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 0 is considered Armstrong (0^1=0). 1 has 1 digit: 1^1=1 → YES.
9474 is a 4‑digit Armstrong number. 548834 is a 6‑digit Armstrong number.

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.expectedOutput:
YES
YES
YES
YES
YES
NO

solution.approach: For each number N we count its decimal digits d. An Armstrong number satisfies sum_{each digit x of N} x^d = N. We compute d by converting N to a string (or by repeated division). Then we iterate over the digits, raise each digit to the power d (using a small loop because d ≤ 7) and accumulate the sum. Finally we compare the sum with the original N. The algorithm runs in O(d) per test case, where d ≤ 7, so it easily meets the limits. No pre‑computation is required.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
